title: Present simple: I/you
question: 'I ___ coffee every morning.' Which is correct?
explanation: With I/you/we/they, use the base verb: 'I drink.' No -s needed.

title: Present simple: he/she/it
question: 'She ___ to work by bus.' Which is correct?
explanation: With he/she/it, add -s or -es: 'She goes.'

title: Present simple: negative
question: 'He ___ like spicy food.' Which is correct?
explanation: With he/she/it, use 'doesn't' + base verb: 'He doesn't like.'

title: Present simple: negative
question: 'They ___ live in London.' Which is correct?
explanation: With they/we/I/you, use 'don't' + base verb: 'They don't live.'

title: Present simple: question
question: '___ she speak French?' Which is correct?
explanation: For questions with he/she/it, start with 'Does': 'Does she speak?'

title: Present simple: question
question: '___ you want some tea?' Which is correct?
explanation: For questions with I/you/we/they, start with 'Do': 'Do you want?'

title: Present simple: he/she/it
question: 'My brother ___ football on Saturdays.' Which is correct?
explanation: With he/she/it (my brother = he), add -s: 'He plays.'

title: Present simple: I/you
question: 'We ___ dinner at seven o'clock.' Which is correct?
explanation: With we/I/you/they, use the base verb: 'We eat.' No -s needed.

title: Present simple: negative
question: 'I ___ understand this word.' Which is correct?
explanation: With I, use 'don't' + base verb: 'I don't understand.'

title: Present continuous: I
question: 'I ___ a book right now.' Which is correct?
explanation: With I, use 'am' + verb-ing: 'I am reading.' It is happening now.

title: Present continuous: she
question: 'She ___ on the phone at the moment.' Which is correct?
explanation: With she/he/it, use 'is' + verb-ing: 'She is talking.' It is happening now.

title: Present continuous: they
question: 'They ___ for the bus outside.' Which is correct?
explanation: With they/we/you, use 'are' + verb-ing: 'They are waiting.'

title: Present continuous: he
question: 'Look! He ___ in the pool.' Which is correct?
explanation: With he/she/it, use 'is' + verb-ing: 'He is swimming.' It is happening now.

title: Present continuous: we
question: 'We ___ lunch in the garden today.' Which is correct?
explanation: With we, use 'are' + verb-ing: 'We are having.' It is happening now.

title: Present continuous: negative
question: 'She ___ right now; she is resting.' Which is correct?
explanation: With she, use 'isn't' + verb-ing: 'She isn't working.'

title: Present continuous: question
question: '___ you coming to the party tonight?' Which is correct?
explanation: For present continuous questions with you, use 'Are': 'Are you coming?'

title: Present continuous: I
question: 'I ___ to music while I cook.' Which is correct?
explanation: With I, use 'am' + verb-ing: 'I am listening.' It is happening right now.

title: Present perfect: have/has
question: 'I ___ this movie before.' Which is correct?
explanation: With I/you/we/they, use 'have' + past participle: 'I have seen.'

title: Present perfect: has
question: 'She ___ three countries this year.' Which is correct?
explanation: With she/he/it, use 'has' + past participle: 'She has visited.'

title: Present perfect: for/since
question: 'We have lived here ___ 2018.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'since' with a specific point in time: 'since 2018.'

title: Present perfect: for/since
question: 'He has worked there ___ five years.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'for' with a period of time: 'for five years.'

title: Present perfect: has
question: 'My sister ___ just passed her driving test.' Which is correct?
explanation: With she/he/it (my sister = she), use 'has' + past participle: 'She has passed.'

title: Present perfect: yet
question: 'I haven't called him back ___.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'yet' at the end of a negative sentence to mean 'up to now': 'I haven't called him back yet.'

title: Present perfect: already
question: 'I have ___ eaten lunch, so I'm not hungry.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'already' in positive sentences to say something happened sooner than expected.

title: Present perfect: just
question: 'He has ___ arrived; he walked in one minute ago.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'just' to say something happened very recently: 'He has just arrived.'

title: Present perfect: negative
question: 'They ___ never been to Japan.' Which is correct?
explanation: With they, use 'have' + never + past participle: 'They have never been.'

title: Possessive pronoun: mine
question: 'This bag is ___. I bought it last week.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Mine' is a possessive pronoun. It replaces 'my bag.' Use it alone, without a noun after it.

title: Possessive pronoun: yours
question: 'Is this phone ___?' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Yours' is a possessive pronoun. It stands alone: 'Is this phone yours?' No noun after it.

title: Possessive pronoun: his
question: 'That bicycle is ___. He rides it to school.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'His' can be a possessive pronoun (standing alone): 'That bicycle is his.'

title: Possessive pronoun: hers
question: 'The red coat is ___. She left it on the chair.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Hers' is a possessive pronoun. No apostrophe needed. It stands alone without a noun.

title: Possessive pronoun: ours
question: 'That garden is ___. We planted the flowers.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Ours' is a possessive pronoun. It replaces 'our garden' and stands alone.

title: Possessive pronoun: theirs
question: 'The house on the corner is ___.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Theirs' is a possessive pronoun. It replaces 'their house' and stands alone.

title: Possessive pronoun: mine
question: 'Your car is blue, but ___ is white.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Mine' replaces 'my car.' Use it alone: 'mine is white.' No noun follows.

title: Possessive pronoun: yours
question: 'My keys are here. Where are ___?' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Yours' replaces 'your keys.' It stands alone. No apostrophe is needed.

title: Possessive adjective: my
question: '___ name is Anny.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'My' is a possessive adjective. It goes before a noun: 'My name.'

title: Possessive adjective: your
question: 'Is this ___ umbrella?' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Your' is a possessive adjective. It goes before a noun: 'your umbrella.'

title: Possessive adjective: his
question: 'He forgot ___ wallet at home.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'His' is a possessive adjective here. It goes before a noun: 'his wallet.'

title: Possessive adjective: her
question: 'She loves ___ new job.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Her' is a possessive adjective. It goes before a noun: 'her new job.'

title: Possessive adjective: our
question: 'We are painting ___ house this weekend.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Our' is a possessive adjective. It goes before a noun: 'our house.'

title: Possessive adjective: their
question: '___ children go to the same school.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Their' is a possessive adjective. It goes before a noun: 'Their children.'

title: Possessive adjective: its
question: 'The cat is licking ___ paw.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Its' is a possessive adjective (no apostrophe). 'It's' means 'it is.' Use 'its' before a noun.

title: Possessive adjective: my
question: 'I always bring ___ lunch to work.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'My' goes before a noun: 'my lunch.' 'Mine' would stand alone without a noun.

title: Adverb of frequency: always
question: 'She ___ brushes her teeth before bed.' Which is correct?
explanation: The adverb goes before the main verb: 'She always brushes.' 'Ever' is for questions and negatives.

title: Adverb of frequency: never
question: 'I ___ eat fast food. I prefer home cooking.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Never' goes before the main verb: 'I never eat.' It already includes the negative — don't add 'not.'

title: Adverb of frequency: usually
question: 'We ___ go to the park on Sundays.' Which is correct?
explanation: The adverb goes before the main verb: 'We usually go.' 'Usual' is an adjective — the adverb form is 'usually.'

title: Adverb of frequency: sometimes
question: 'He ___ works late on Fridays.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Sometimes' is the adverb: 'He sometimes works late.' 'Occasional' is an adjective; 'sometime' means 'at an unknown time.'

title: Adverb of frequency: often
question: 'They ___ visit their grandparents.' Which is correct?
explanation: The adverb goes before the main verb: 'They often visit.' 'Much' and 'many' are not adverbs of frequency.

title: Adverb of frequency: rarely
question: 'She ___ watches television.' Which is correct?
explanation: The adverb goes before the main verb: 'She rarely watches.' 'Rare' is an adjective — the adverb form is 'rarely.'

title: Adverb of frequency: with be
question: 'He is ___ late for class.' Which is correct?
explanation: With the verb 'be,' the adverb goes after it: 'He is always late.' 'Ever' is for questions and negatives.

title: Adverb of frequency: with be
question: 'They are ___ happy to help.' Which is correct?
explanation: With the verb 'be,' the adverb goes after it: 'They are usually happy.' 'Usual' and 'normal' are adjectives, not adverbs.
